abstract: Porous materials are essential for industrial gas separations, offering a unique platform where adsorbent properties can be precisely engineered to target specific molecules. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have emerged as a versatile class of porous solids, providing nearly endless opportunities to design materials with tailored functionalities. Because the structural possibilities are so vast, quantum mechanical computations have become an indispensable tool for prioritizing the synthesis of the most promising candidates. In this study, we employ electronic structure calculations to predict a new metal-organic framework specifically designed for the separation of dinitrogen from methane—a technically demanding process that is vital for the purification and utilization of natural gas. Our results indicate that incorporating open V(II) sites into a MOF creates an environment where dinitrogen is adsorbed with a significantly higher enthalpy than methane. This high selectivity is driven by strong π-backbonding between the vanadium center and dinitrogen, an interaction that is fundamentally absent for methane.
